full_response:
We want to show that the apparently “strange’’ line element  

ds² = –dt² + α² t² dx² + dy² + dz²                                (1)

is nothing more than the ordinary flat–space Minkowski metric  

ds² = –dT² + dX² + dY² + dZ²                                     (2)

written in unusual coordinates.  
Because the y– and z–directions already match, we restrict attention to 1 + 1 dimensions:

(ds²)1+1 = –dt² + α² t² dx².                                     (3)

Our task is therefore to find a change of variables (T(t,x), X(t,x)) such that

–dT² + dX² = –dt² + α² t² dx².                                   (4)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Ansatz for the coordinate transformation  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Motivated by the hint a² – b² = 1 and the identity cosh²β – sinh²β = 1, try

T = t cosh(αx),                                             (5a)  
X = t sinh(αx).                                             (5b)

These are precisely analogous to the “Milne-Rindler’’ coordinates that cover the interior of the future light-cone in Minkowski space.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Compute the differentials  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Introduce C ≡ cosh(αx) and S ≡ sinh(αx) for compactness.  Then

dT = C dt + tαS dx,                                         (6a)  
dX = S dt + tαC dx.                                         (6b)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Plug into the Minkowski line element  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Compute –dT² + dX²:

–dT² = –[C² dt² + 2tαCS dt dx + t²α²S² dx²],  
 dX² =  S² dt² + 2tαCS dt dx + t²α²C² dx².

Adding them,

–dT² + dX²  
= (–C² + S²) dt² + (–2tαCS + 2tαCS) dt dx + t²α²(C² – S²) dx²  
= [S² – C²] dt²                (cross–term cancels)  
  + t²α²[C² – S²] dx².

Use cosh²β – sinh²β = 1 ⇒ C² – S² = 1 and S² – C² = –1 :

–dT² + dX² = –dt² + α² t² dx².                             (7)

This is exactly the desired form (3).  Hence the line element (1) is flat; it is simply the ordinary Minkowski metric, written in the coordinates (t,x,y,z) defined by (5).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Inverse transformation (optional check)  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Solving (5) for (t,x) gives

t = √(T² – X²),                     (T > |X|),             (8a)  
x = (1/α) artanh(X/T).                                     (8b)

These formulae are well-defined inside the light-cone T² > X² and one can verify directly that substituting them back into (2) again yields (1).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Conclusion  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The metric  

ds² = –dt² + α² t² dx² + dy² + dz²

is merely Minkowski spacetime described in the coordinates (t,x,y,z) related to standard Cartesian coordinates (T,X,Y,Z) by  

T = t cosh(αx),    X = t sinh(αx),    Y = y,    Z = z.

Therefore no new physics is implied; the spacetime is flat.

\boxed{\;T = t\cosh(\alpha x), \; X = t\sinh(\alpha x)\; \Longrightarrow\;
-dT^{2}+dX^{2} = -dt^{2}+\alpha^{2}t^{2}dx^{2}\;}

Confidence: 83%
